_meta.source: AI Phase 7 missing-verse assignment via Claude API (Sonnet 4.6)

assignments.rationale: Jacob's awe upon waking is the inner event of dread triggered by unexpected divine nearness — reverential alarm at a holy place, fitting VCG-04's awe-overwhelm at divine display.
The 'Fear of Isaac' as divine name identifies reverential awe as the defining, enduring inner orientation of Isaac's life — a generational, identity-constituting posture, fitting VCG-06.
Jacob swears by the Fear of Isaac, invoking ancestral reverence as the ground of solemn oath — the transmitted, generational fear-of-God as covenant identity marker fits VCG-06.

Pharaoh's servants who feared God's word acted immediately to protect dependents — inner God-directed reverence functions as operative ethical constraint producing just, protective action, fitting VCG-01.
Socially instilled dread of divine judgment deters future idolatry — fear functions as internal restraint against wrongdoing produced by witnessing public justice, fitting VCG-01.
Fear produced by public punishment of presumption curbs arrogance; the inner restraint from witnessing judgment prevents future defiance — fear as ethical constraint fits VCG-01.
Punishment of false witness instills fear that restrains others from similar evil — the deterrent inner response of dread functions as ethical constraint on wrongdoing, fitting VCG-01.
The priest commands soldiers not to yield to fear before battle — this is redirection away from misplaced fear (of enemies) toward trust, the characteristic phenomenon of VCG-05.
Stoning of a rebellious son produces communal dread that restrains lawlessness — fear as social-moral deterrent against wrongdoing functions as ethical constraint fitting VCG-01.
Saul's awe-laden dread of David is triggered by recognizing a power beyond the merely human in David's extraordinary success — the inner event of awe-overwhelm at a divine display fits VCG-04.
All Israel stood in awe of Solomon perceiving divine wisdom in his judgment — reverential fear triggered by recognizing God's presence and power in a human act fits VCG-04.
Solomon prays for a sustained fear of God governing the people's whole life in the land — enduring, life-ordering inner orientation defining covenant relationship fits VCG-06.
Solomon prays that foreigners hearing of God may come to fear him as Israel does — fear divinely extended to foreign peoples through the knowledge of God's name fits VCG-07.
Fear of the Chaldeans drove mass flight to Egypt — this is misplaced fear of a human enemy overriding covenant faithfulness, exemplifying misplaced fear VCG-05 addresses.
David's immediate fear after witnessing Uzzah's death is the inner awe-event triggered by witnessing divine judgment — reverential dread evoked by God's displayed power fits VCG-04.
The Lord's incomparable greatness makes him uniquely worthy of reverential awe above all gods — fear grounded in God's own incomparable nature and greatness fits VCG-04.
Enemies' fear upon seeing the wall completed arose from recognizing God's hand — reverential dread instilled in foreign opponents by God's historical deed fits VCG-07.
Job's terror of divine calamity shaped his moral behavior throughout life — dread of God functioning as an internal restraint governing ethical conduct fits VCG-01.
Absence of dread of God in the wicked's inner vision enables transgression — the lack of God-fear as moral restraint allowing wrongdoing directly mirrors VCG-01's operative constraint.
The king's awesome deeds in battle inspire awe-filled fear in others — the fearsome power perceived in his majesty evokes the reverential dread characteristic of VCG-04.

Fear of God is the goal that God's confirmed word is meant to produce — the psalmist treats sustained fear as the defining orientation of devotion to God that God's faithfulness nurtures, fitting VCG-06.
The wise are commanded not to fear sudden terror — this redirects inner disposition away from dread of the wicked toward trust in God, the characteristic redirection of fear in VCG-05.
Revering God's commandment as inner disposition leads to reward and contrasts with despising the word — reverential fear expressed as obedient relationship constraining conduct fits VCG-01.
Hezekiah's fear of the Lord expressed as prayer that averted disaster — the inner reverence as relational posture opening to God's saving response and provision fits VCG-03.

Uriah's fear of the king's murderous intent drove flight to Egypt — this is fear of a human threat (misplaced, overwhelming) rather than God-directed reverence, fitting VCG-05's misplaced fear category.
Herod's fear of John grounded in recognition of his holiness operated protectively in his will, constraining him from harming John — fear functioning as an internal ethical restraint fits VCG-01.
Walking in the fear of the Lord as the sustained inner orientation governing church life and growth — enduring, community-defining reverence constitutive of faithful identity fits VCG-06.
Not fearing what is frightening is the inner freedom of Sarah's daughters who do good — the positive freedom from human fear through trust in God is the characteristic theme of VCG-05.
Moses directly commands Israel not to fear as the Egyptians close in, countering inner panic with divine assurance of imminent saving action.
Reverential awe before God's intimate relationship with Moses should have restrained Miriam and Aaron — fear here is the proper inner response to divine transcendence.
God directly commands Moses not to fear Og, countering inward dread of a formidable enemy king with divine assurance of victory.
Fear as an inner disposition rendering soldiers unfit for battle — reactive dread before a military threat that disqualifies those who possess it.
Fear as an inner impediment governing obedient action; God accommodates it by providing an alternative path, showing fear drives or constrains personal decision-making.
Boaz's involuntary startled alarm at an unexpected physical presence in the dark — reactive inner jolt triggered by a perceived threat.
The women's 'do not be afraid' directly addresses and attempts to counter the dying mother's acute inner anguish, though grief overwhelms the assurance.
The elders tremble at Samuel's unexpected arrival, fearing divine judgment — an inner apprehension of danger expressed bodily before he speaks.
Ahimelech's trembling at David's unexpected solitary arrival is reactive inner alarm at a signal of danger from a prominent threatening figure.
David's dread of the Lord after Uzzah's death is an overwhelming encounter with divine holiness and judgment that redirects his will and halts his action.
Future security framed as absence of inner disturbance from violent oppressors — the fear addressed is reactive dread triggered by human military threats.
Isaiah commands Hezekiah's servants not to fear Assyria's threatening words — divine word directly counters inward dread triggered by enemy speech.
God promises freedom from inner and outer disturbance caused by violent oppressors — reactive dread of human military threat is what will be eliminated.
Fear of human enemies is directly addressed and countered by redirecting inner dread toward the great and awesome Lord — a commanded release of acute fear.
Freedom from fear as the inner fruit of moral integrity — fear governs personal standing before God and is resolved through restored innocence.
God's majesty and dread as an overwhelming inner weight confronting the friends — dread produced by direct encounter with divine transcendence compelling humility.
Dread as an internal experience haunting the wicked man's conscience, anticipating destruction — fear governing the inner person through guilt and anticipated judgment.
Sudden terror overwhelming Job framed as consequence of moral entrapment — dread strikes the inner person like a flood, triggered by surrounding snares.
The war horse laughs at dread and does not retreat — dread here is reactive fear before battle terrors; its absence marks fearless inner disposition under threat.
Sudden great dread overtakes the wicked caused by God's presence with the righteous — inner terror produced by confronting divine reality aligned against them.
The wicked are seized by panic precisely where God is present among the righteous — inner terror triggered by encountering the reality of divine alignment.
With God as stronghold, the psalmist asks of whom to be in dread — inner terror toward human threats is rendered irrational by divine protective presence.
The psalmist has become an object of dread to acquaintances — dread operates as social and relational rupture, a fear of association with his suffering.
Great dread seizes God's opponents even without genuine threat — groundless panic caused by divine rejection when confronting the reality of God's presence with his people.
The wicked are in great irrational dread where no real danger exists — inner terror self-produced as consequence of God's rejection, a panic before divine reality.
Absence of fear as moral failure — enemies act without inner restraint or reverential hesitation, showing fear here functions as social-moral check on harmful action.
Israel did not experience dread under God's guidance — absence of inner fear before enemies is the evidence of divine protection against military threat.
Isaiah commanded not to adopt the people's communal fear — the inner disposition of dread is directly named, addressed, and countered by divine instruction.
God commands Zion not to fear the Assyrians — the divine word directly addresses and counters inner dread triggered by an intimidating enemy's threat.
Coastlands see God's action and are afraid — inward recoil at witnessing divine power at work, recognizing they stand before transcendent reality beyond their control.
Dream-produced alarm strikes the inner person directly — a supernatural/divine-origin dread overwhelming the mind, fitting the involuntary inner response to transcendent encounter.
Reactive anxious dread directed at a specific object (idol calf's fate), episodic and object-directed fear gripping the community at perceived threat of loss.
Storm-driven mortal terror grips the mariners, redirecting action toward the divine — reactive dread triggered by a life-threatening physical situation.
Fear intensifies upon learning Jonah flees the Lord who made the sea — the terror escalates specifically from recognition of divine identity, fitting supernatural-encounter dread.
Nations cringe and creep in abject terror before the Lord — the collapse into serpent-like retreat is the involuntary bodily-spiritual response to confronting divine power.
Babylonians are fearsome because they are a self-sovereign armed threat answering to no restraint — reactive dread of a dangerous human military power.
Ashkelon is struck with fear as divine judgment falls on neighboring cities, recoiling as mortal threat draws near its own borders — episodic reactive dread at approaching destruction.
The great and awesome day of the Lord evokes dread of direct divine intervention — the terror is produced by confronting transcendent divine reality about to break in.
Angel commands Joseph not to fear — the fear is directly named and countered by divine word of assurance, the classic commanded release of acute fear by external authoritative address.
Centurion and guards respond with awe to supernatural signs at Jesus' death, producing confession of his identity — NT pattern of fear as precursor to recognition of who Jesus is.
Jesus commands Jairus not to fear but believe — fear is directly named and countered by Jesus' word of assurance at the moment of greatest threat.
Fear of social/authority consequences silences the disciples' question to Jesus — inner apprehension governing speech to avoid perceived judgment from one in authority.
Dread grips followers on the road to Jerusalem at what lies ahead — reactive inner fear of mortal threat governing their inner state as they approach danger.
Paralysing awe-terror at the empty tomb and angelic encounter silences and drives the women to flight — involuntary overwhelming response to direct supernatural encounter.
Jesus directly names and counters Jairus's fear with the command not to fear but believe — commanded release of acute fear grounded in Jesus' power and promise.
Fear of social/authority judgment silences disciples' inquiry — inner apprehension governing speech to avoid perceived rebuke from Jesus, a social-authority fear dynamic.
Cosmic signs produce fear so overwhelming it causes fainting — dread of transcendent catastrophic divine intervention overwhelming the body, fitting supernatural-encounter terror.
Women are called not to yield to frightening alarm (ptoēsis) — the commanded non-surrender to acute fear grounds courage in doing good, fitting the pattern of fear directly addressed and countered.
Survivors are terrified by catastrophic earthquake and give glory to God — extreme dread as involuntary response to divine power manifest in catastrophe, producing acknowledgment of divine authority.
Kings stand far off in self-protective dread of sharing Babylon's punishment — fear governs their physical distancing and inaction, a fear-driven personal decision to stay away for self-preservation.
Merchants' self-interested dread of judgment governs their physical distance and shapes their behavior — fear driving personal inaction and flight from association with the condemned.
God's movement against Assyria causes even its most solid stronghold to dissolve in terror — the terror is a function of divine majesty rising in judgment, not a weapon deployed ahead of Israel.
Terror arrives unexpectedly, overtaking and overwhelming the inner person where peace was anticipated — the relentless, overtaking character of the terror matches VCG-02's hunting/flooding agency.

Terror replaces hoped-for healing, overwhelming the soul with shock under divine judgment — the inner overtaking, inescapable dismay fits VCG-02's emphasis on terror as an overwhelming encircling force.
Benjamin's collapse is panic-driven flight triggered by perceiving imminent disaster—inner agitation dissolving military resolve, fitting the ba.hal panic-flight meaning.
Social dread paralyzes Job's will and suppresses action—inner collapse of composure from an external threat (public contempt), leaving him speechless and confined.
Comprehending the judgment message itself produces sheer trembling horror—dismay arising from receiving/grasping terrible news, inner collapse from understanding catastrophe.
The wise are dismayed and taken as direct consequence of rejecting God's word—divine judgment actively producing inner collapse in those who opposed divine wisdom.
Absence of military fear-trembling as covenant peace gift; the threat context is enemy attack, making this a battle-fear register even in its negated form.
Prohibition of dread before enemies in military context; inner trembling-terror before enemy forces that God fights, classic battle-fear register.
Command not to tremble before nations; military context with God's awesome presence as counter to battle-dread spreading through the camp.
Pre-battle exhortation explicitly forbidding trembling-dread that collapses soldiers' resolve; textbook battle-fear-alarm VCG-04 context.
Soldiers forbidden to shrink in inner dread before enemies; God's presence is the antidote to the contagious battle-fear trembling.
Joshua commanded not to tremble in dread; military leadership context, God's universal presence counters the inner collapse of battle-fear.
Absence of threat-driven trembling as mark of divine security; no enemy will make Job afraid—negated military-social fear register parallels Lev 26:6.
God frightens Job as a frail driven leaf—visceral flesh-level dread imposed by overwhelming divine power on a helpless sufferer; somatic register dominates.
Inner terror of the crowd's opinion that physically unmans Job; visceral dread registering in the body overriding courageous action—flesh-level shuddering.
Oppressor's power to impose terror on vulnerable others; God's justice ends this capacity—social-military dread-production in battle/power-threat context.
Lord rising to terrify the earth drives people into caves; whole-body convulsion before divine majesty in theophanic context—classic VCG-01 register.
Same theophanic event as 2:19; Lord's splendor and majesty overwhelms human composure—people flee into rock-clefts, involuntary whole-body response to holiness.
Prophet commanded to resist crowd's contagious dread; this is social-military fear-trembling spreading through the community that must not govern the prophet.
Redirecting inner trembling from human threats to God alone—chosen reverential trembling before the holy Lord as identity-forming stance, VCG-02 tradition.
People stand in reverential awe of the God of Israel upon seeing his redemptive work; chosen reverential trembling before divine holiness—VCG-02 register.
Prophet commands trembling as wake-up response to coming disaster; trembling at witnessing/anticipating catastrophic destruction—VCG-03 witnessing-destruction register.
Babylon challenged to inspire terror in enemies via sorcery; capacity to produce awe-dread in opponents is a military-power register—VCG-04 battle-fear context.
Ephraim's speech induced trembling awe in others; visceral involuntary dread-response to commanding authority, flesh-level shuddering produced by overwhelming presence.
Trumpet alarm produces communal trembling before approaching day of the Lord; witnessing/anticipating overwhelming divine catastrophe—VCG-03 register.
Eschatological peace defined by absence of threat-induced trembling; military/enemy-fear register negated as the ideal future, paralleling Lev 26:6.
Nations come trembling from strongholds toward the Lord; whole-body collapse before divine sovereign authority—theophanic encounter producing involuntary trembling.
Tents of Cushan tremble at God's theophanic approach; visible shaking externalizing involuntary whole-body response to divine theophanic manifestation.
Fear of death as a lifelong, all-pervasive inner slavery saturating daily existence fits VCG-01's sustained, constant companion dread rather than a specific anticipated event.
